questionText: The masses, $X$ grams, of apples from an orchard are modelled by $X \sim N(160,\ \sigma^2)$, where $\sigma$ is unknown. It is found that $20\%$ of the apples have a mass less than $145$ g. Find the value of $\sigma$, giving your answer to $3$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and translate the information
steps.explanation: The masses are normal with mean $160$ and unknown standard deviation. ‘$20\%$ have a mass less than $145$ g’ gives the lower-tail probability $P(X<145)=0.20$.
The standard-normal value with $20\%$ of the area below it is $-0.8416$, negative because $145$ lies below the mean $160$.
Standardising $145$ must give this $z$. The mean is known, so $\sigma$ is the only unknown in the equation.
The mass $145$ is $15$ g below the mean; as a multiple of $\sigma$ this negative distance equals the negative $z$-score.
The negatives on both sides cancel, leaving a positive distance of $15$ g equal to $0.8416$ standard deviations.
If $15$ g equals $0.8416$ standard deviations, then one standard deviation is $15$ divided by $0.8416$.
Dividing gives the standard deviation in grams.

steps.description: State the answer to 3 significant figures
steps.explanation: To three significant figures the standard deviation of the apple masses is $17.8$ g.

steps.description: Check the answer reproduces the given probability
steps.explanation: Standardising $145$ with $\sigma=17.8$ returns about $-0.843$, whose lower-tail area is $0.20$, confirming the answer.
